coredata.dc:title: Experimental study of a passive thermal management system using copper foam-paraffin composite for lithium ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhuqian
Li, Yi
coredata.dc:description: A passive thermal management system using copper metal foam saturated with phase change materials is designed for LiFePO4 battery pack. Copper metal foam is aimed to enhance low thermal conductivity of PCM and improve temperature uniformity of the battery pack. The thermal efficiency of the system is experimentally investigated and compared with two dissipation patterns of air cooling and pure PCM. The results show that the dissipation pattern of the foam-PCM composite can significantly reduce the battery’s surface temperature compared with the other two patterns. Moreover, the foam-PCM composite can bring the battery pack a better temperature uniformity, especially during the higher discharge rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery thermal management
Phase change material
Copper metal foam

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Management, Policy, Economics and Sustainability

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 24 3 24 8 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.174 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.174 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 9th International Conference on Applied Energy, ICAE2017, 21-24 August 2017, Cardiff, UK Experimental study of a passive thermal management system using copper foam-paraffin composite for lithium ion batteries Zhuqian Zhanga,b,*, Yi Lia,b aInstitute of Thermal Engineering, School of Mechanical, Electronic and Control Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China bBeijing Key Laboratory of Flow and Heat Transfer of Phase Changing in Micro and Small Scale, Beijing 100044, China Abstract A passive thermal management system using copper metal foam saturated with phase change materials is designed for LiFePO4 battery pack. Copper metal foam is aimed to enhance low thermal conductivity of PCM and improve temperature uniformity of the battery pack. The thermal efficiency of the system is experimentally investigated and compared with two dissipation patterns of air cooling and pure PCM. The results show that the dissipation pattern of the foam-PCM composite can significantly reduce the batteryâ€™s surface temperature compared with the other two patterns. Moreover, the foam-PCM composite can bring the battery pack a better temperature uniformity, especially during the higher discharge rate. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. Keywords: Battery thermal management; Phase change material; Copper metal foam 1. Introduction Recently, lithium-ion batt ri s sed as electric vehicle pow r supply have raised peopleâ€™s concern due to their high power density, stable charge and di charge cycle and relatively long life span. It has been reported that the optimum operating temperatures for Li-ion batteries should range from 20 to 50 Â°C, and the maximum temperature difference in a battery pack should be kept within 5 Â°C[1]. The battery temperature during the charge and discharge process must be maintained within an allowable temperature range. Additionally, battery packs usually consist of many cells * Zhuqian Zhang. Tel.: +010-5168-4329; fax: +010-5168-4329. E-mail address: zhqzhang@bjtu.edu.cn Available online at .sciencedirect.co i ir t Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 9th International Conference on Applied Energy. 9th International onference on pplied nergy, I 2017, 21-24 ugust 2017, ardiff, ri t l t f i t r l t t i r f - r ffi it f r lit i i tt ri ia a a,b, , i ia,b aInstitute of Thermal Engineering, School of echanical, Electronic and Control Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China bBeijing Key Laboratory of Flow and Heat Transfer of Phase Changing in icro and Small Scale, Beijing 100044, China bstract passive ther al anage ent syste using copper etal foa saturated ith phase change aterials is designed for LiFeP 4 battery pack. opper etal foa is ai ed to enhance lo ther al conductivity of P and i prove te perature unifor ity of the battery pack. The ther al efficiency of the syste is experi entally investigated and co pared ith t o dissipation patterns of air cooling and pure P . The results sho that the dissipation pattern of the foa -P co posite can significantly reduce the batteryâ€™s surface te perature co pared ith the other t o patterns. oreover, the foa -P co posite can bring the battery pack a better te perature unifor ity, especially during the higher discharge rate. 2017 The uthors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-revie under responsibility of the scientific co ittee of the 9th International Conference on pplied Energy. Keywords: Battery thermal management; Phase change material; Copper metal foam 1. Introduction ecently, lithiu -ion batteries used as electric vehicle po er supply have raised peopleâ€™s concern due to their high po er density, stable charge and discharge cycle and relatively long life span. It has been reported that the opti u op rating te peratur s for Li-ion batteries should range fro 20 to 50 Â° , and the axi u te perature differ nce in a bat ery pack should be k pt ithin 5 Â° [1]. The battery te p rature during the charge and ischarge rocess ust be aintained ithin an allo able te perature range. dditionally, battery packs usually consist of any cells * Zhuqian Zhang. Tel.: +010-5168-4329; fax: +010-5168-4329. E-mail address: zhqzhang bjtu.edu.cn 2404 Zhuqian Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2403â€“2408 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 connected in series and parallel and the temperature uniformity among cells was crucial to the battery packâ€™s safety[2, 3]. Therefore, a very efficient thermal management system is necessary for a high-powered Li-ion battery module. Traditional thermal management methods such as air cooling [4-6] and liquid cooling [7-9]have been extensively investigated and adopted on commercial EVs in recent years. However, those heat dissipation modes are generally complicated and bulky and power-wasting components diminish the limited energy stored in the batteries. These undesirable disadvantages provoke expectations for novel thermal management systems. Recently, a kind of passive thermal management using phase change material (PCM) has been considered as an alternative to the aforementioned traditional system. Zhonghao Rao et al.[10]discussed the thermal energy management performance of ageing commercial rectangular LiFePO4 power batteries using phase change material. The results showed that the PCM with a melting point lower than 45Â°C will be more effective for heat dissipation, with a desired maximum temperature below 50 Â°C. A passive thermal management system for high-power Li-ion packs compared with a purely air-cooling mode was proposed by Riza Kizilel et al[11]. They found that a passive thermal management system utilizing PCM provides faster heat dissipation than active cooling during high pulse power discharges. To resolve the conflict of large heat storage capacity and low thermal conductivity of traditional PCM, composite PCM have been widely used such as carbon fiber[12], metal foam or expanded graphite into the paraffin[13, 14]. A two-dimensional transient model for a passive thermal management system using the phase change material (PCM) of paraffin saturated in metallic copper foam was developed by Z.G. Qu et al[15]. It can be seen from the result that the thermal management by foam-PCM composite has dramatically reduced battery surface temperature to the allowable range at 1C and 3C discharge rates compared to the air convection and adiabatic modes. Angelo Greco et al.[16]investigated a thermal management of a cylindrical battery cell by a phase change material (PCM)/compressed expanded natural graphite (CENG). The result showed that the maximum temperature reached by the PCM/CENG cooling strategy is much lower than that by the forced convection in the same configuration and the PCM showed superior transient characteristics to forced convection cooling. From previous works, studies investigating the thermal management of batteries using foam-PCM composites have been inadequate. Moreover, comparison of temperature uniformity with different dissipation modes and the effects of discharge rate on the max temperature difference and rise of batteries are rarely mentioned in previous works. In this study, a passive thermal dissipation system for LiFePO4 high-energy batteries using the integration of copper foam and paraffin is designed. Two additional thermal dissipation modes, including air natural convection and a pure PCM, are also used as reference cases. The thermal behaviors of the system are evaluated by comparing the batteryâ€™s surface temperature using the three dissipation modes described above. 2. Materials preparation and characterization Commercial paraffin is used as the organic PCM. The thermal properties of the paraffin are measured using a Differential Scanning Calorimeter. The fusion point is from 35 Â°C to 40 Â°C, the thermal conductivity is 0.21 W/mâˆ™k and the latent heat is 269 kJ/kg. Thermal properties of paraffin are shown in Table 1. The copper foam is supplied by Hebei metal materials company. The porosity (Îµ) and pore density (Ï‰, PPI: pore number per inch) of the foam sample used in the experiment are 0.9 and 50, respectively. The paraffin is heated to 80 Â°C. After complete melting, the liquid paraffin is infiltrated into the pores of the copper foam. The foam-paraffin composite is completed after air cooled. Table 1. Thermo-physical properties of paraffin. Parameters Value Density (kg m-3) Latent heat (kJ kg-1) Thermal conductivity (W m-1 K-1) Specific heat capacity (J kg-1 K-1) Kinetic viscosity (Pa s) 800 180 0.23 3000 0.00441 Zhuqian Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2403â€“2408 2405 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3 3. Experimental setup and procedure 3.1. Experimental system Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the experimental system. It includes three modules: the testing section, the charging and discharging equipment and the data acquisition instrument. A BTS-4000 battery test station (BTS- 60V100A) is supplied to perform the charge/discharge process of the battery pack at indoor temperature. T-type thermocouples are applied to track the battery surface temperature. Fig. 1. Sketch of the experimental system. All the thermocouples are connected to a data acquisition device. 3.2. Battery pack Industrial LiFePO4 battery (70Ã—100Ã—27 mm3, Tianjin Lishen Battery Joint-Stock Co., Ltd) with capacity of 13.5 Ah is used in this experiment. Electrical characteristics of the Li-ion cell are shown in Table 2. The thermal management of the foam-paraffin composite is shown in Fig. 2(a). The battery pack is constituted by ten foam-paraffin pieces and nine batteries in the pattern of a compact sandwich structure. The batteries are assembled in a series connection. Meanwhile, for comparision, another thermal management module with pure PCM is also assembled with a similar configuration, as depicted in Fig. 2(b). Table 2. Electrical characteristics of the Li-ion cell. Parameters Value Capacity (Ah) Length (mm) Width (mm) Thickness (mm) Nominal voltage (V) Cell pack voltage (V) Max discharge current (A) 13.5 100 70 27 3.65 32.85 81(6C) In addition, the batteries are assembled in a series connection without anything in the inter-cell gaps to investigate another factor of air natural convection. (a) (b) Fig. 2. Battery pack design (a) using pure paraffin; (b) using copper foam-paraffin composite 3.3. Testing The battery packs are tested at a constant room temperature of 21 Â°C. During the experiment process, the battery packs are subjected to the following charge rule: Galvanostatic mode at 0.5 C rate with a voltage cut-off limit of 18 V and then a potentiostatic mode until the current reduces to 0.675 A. After the charging process, the battery packs are 2406 Zhuqian Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2403â€“2408 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 cooled to the room temperature. Then, the battery packs are discharged at different rate of 2, 3, 4 and 5 C. All the thermocouples with an accuracy of Â± 0.1 Â°C are attached to the battery surface. Five thermocouples are mounted on the surface of each battery, as shown in Fig. 3. (a) (b) Fig. 3. (a) three-dimension model of battery pack; (b) position of thermocouple. The temperature of each cell is obtained by the average value of the five thermocouples. The highest and lowest temperature of the whole battery pack are achieved by the maximum value and the minimum value among all the thermocouples, respectively. The difference between the highest temperature of the battery pack and the initial battery temperature is considered as the max temperature rise. The difference between the highest and lowest temperature of the whole battery pack is considered as the max temperature difference. 4. Results and discussions 4.1. Comparison of dissipation patterns The surface temperature of battery during the discharge process is only discussed in this section. Fig. 4 shows the temperature variations with time for the cases of the natural air convection, the pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite at various discharge rates. The original environmental temperature is approximately 21 Â°C. The surface temperature profiles of Cell 5 in the battery pack at 2C-discharge rate are shown in Fig. 4(a). The highest temperature for air cooling is 41.19 Â°C, However, the highest temperature for cases of pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite are 34.1 Â°C and 34.8 Â°C, respectively. Thus, the result indicates that the paraffin temperature donâ€™t reach the melting range during the discharge process of 2C rate. Moreover, as for the two cases of pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite, there is a little difference between the two temperature curves. In terms of the 3C-discharge rate, the comparative results of the battery surface temperature with three dissipation modes are depicted in Fig. 4(b). The temperature with air natural convection reaches 45.2 Â°C. By contrast, the temperature for the cases of pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite finally reach 41.279 Â°C and 38.99 Â°C, respectively. Obviously, the paraffin melted in the case of the pure PCM and the foam-PCM composite at this discharge rate. It can be seen that the temperature rising rate for the foam-PCM composite slowed down apparently within the melting temperature range compared with the pure PCM. And the max surface temperature for the foam-paraffin composite decrease by 6 % and 13.5 % compared with the surface temperature for the pure PCM and air cooling, respectively. Fig. 4. Temperature variations with time under three dissipation modes (a) for the 2C discharge rate; (b) for the 3C discharge rate; (c) for the 4C discharge rate; and (d) for the 5C discharge rate. The battery surface temperature variations for the three cases at 4C-discharge rate and 5C-discharge rate are shown in Fig. 4(c) and Fig. 4(d), respectively. For the 4C discharge rate, the surface temperature for the air natural convection is 54.78 Â°C, the temperature for the cases of pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite increase to 47.411 Â°C and 43.664 Â°C, respectively. Therefore, the latter decrease by 7.9% compared with the former. For the 5C discharge rate, the surface temperature for the air natural convection is 74.69 Â°C exceeding the limited point of 65 Â°C. The temperature Zhuqian Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2403â€“2408 2407 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 5 for the cases of pure PCM and foam-paraffin composite finally reach 54.174 Â°C and 47.86 Â°C, respectively. So the surface temperature for the foam-paraffin composite decreases by 12% compared with the surface temperature for the pure PCM. These results indicate that the combination of paraffin and metal foam can effectively reduce the surface temperature of the battery pack. 4.2. Comparison of temperature uniformity In terms of the safety of the li-ion battery, the temperature uniformity of the whole battery pack is a significant indicator. The uniformity index is defined by the temperature difference (Î”T) between cell 5 and cell 9 as follows: Î”T = ð�‘‡ð�‘‡9 âˆ’ ð�‘‡ð�‘‡5 (1) It can be seen from the equation (1) that a lower value of Î”T reveals a better temperature uniformity of the whole battery pack. The temperature difference comparison of two thermal management patterns at different discharge rates are shown in Fig. 5. On the whole, the temperature difference (Î”T) increase along with the increase of discharge rate during the discharge process. As for the 3C-discharge rate, the value of Î”T with pure paraffin is slightly similar to the Î”T with foam-paraffin composite. At the higher discharge rate of 4C and 5C, the value of Î”T and the Î”T-increasing rate for the foam-PCM composite are lower than the case of pure paraffin. Fig. 5. Comparison of temperature difference for dissipation modes at different discharge rates. This implies that the foam-PCM composite could bring the battery pack a better temperature uniformity, especially at high discharge rate. 4.3. Effect of discharge rate The suitable operating temperature range of Li-ion battery is 18~50 Â°C, so, the max temperature rise could not go beyond 30 Â°C when the environment initial temperature is 22 Â°C, and the max temperature difference among cells could not be over 5 Â°C. Fig. 6(a) shows the comparison results of max temperature rise for the cases of pure PCM and foam-PCM composite with different discharge rates and Fig. 6(b) shows the comparison results of max temperature difference for the two cases. Fig.6. (a) Max temperature rise variations with discharge rate for dissipation modes; (b) Max temperature difference variations with discharge rate for dissipation modes. It can be seen that the max temperature rise and max temperature difference increase along with the increase in discharge rate. Compared with the pure paraffin, the max temperature rise and difference for the foam-PCM composite all decrease markedly. For instance, at the 3C-discharge rate, the max temperature rise and difference for the case of pure PCM are 24.575 Â°C and 2.23 Â°C, respectively. However, the max temperature rise and difference for the foam- PCM composite are 20.663 Â°C and 1.69 Â°C, respectively. The latter are 15.9% and 24.2% lower than the former, 2408 Zhuqian Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 142 (2017) 2403â€“2408 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 respectively. Moreover, at the 3C-discharge rate, the max temperature rise and difference for the two patterns all meet security temperature requirements, nevertheless, at the 5C-discharge rate, both the max temperature rise for the two patterns donâ€™t meet security requirements and only the max temperature difference for the foam-PCM composite satisfies the requirements of battery safety temperature. It is indicated that the pattern of foam-PCM composite could preferably meet security temperature requirements at high discharge rate. 5. Conclusion A highly efficiency thermal management system using copper metal foam saturated with phase change materials is designed and experimentally investigated. The experimental results show that the thermal management mode of the foam-PCM composite reveals better performance of heat dissipation and temperature uniformity compared with air- cooling and pure PCM pattern. For instance, at 5C-dicharge rate, the max temperature for foam-PCM composite is much lower than that of pure PCM (47.86 Â°C v.s. 54.115 Â°C). By contrast, the foam-PCM composite also shows a slower Î”T-increasing rate during the discharge process, especially at high discharge rate. In addition, the dissipation mode of foam-PCM composite can effectively reduce the max temperature rise and difference and it can better meet security temperature requirements of high discharge rate. Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank for support of this work by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51376018). References [1] A. VÃ¤yrynen, J. Salminen. Lithium ion battery production. The Journal of Chemical Thermodynamics, 2012, 46: 80-85. [2] R. Kizilel, A. Lateef, R. Sabbah, et al. Passive control of temperature excursion and uniformity in high-energy Li-ion battery packs at high current and ambient temperature. Journal of Power Sources, 2008, 183(1): 370-375. [3] Ahmad A. Zhao, et al. Thermal investigation of lithium-ion battery module with different cell arrangement structures and forced air-cooling strategies. Applied Energy, 2014, 134: 229-238. [6] H. Park. A design of air flow configuration for cooling lithium ion battery in hybrid electric vehicles. Journal of Power Sources, 2013, 239: 30- 36. [7] Z. Rao, Q. Wang,C. Huang. Investigation of the thermal performance of phase change material/mini-channel coupled battery thermal management system. Applied Energy, 2016, 164: 659-669. [8] L. W. Jin, P. S. Lee, X. X. Kong, et al.Ultra-thin minichannel LCP for EV battery thermal management. Applied Energy, 2014, 113: 1786-1794. [9] T. M. Bandhauer, S. Garimella,T. F. Fuller. A Critical Review of Thermal Issues in Lithium-Ion Batteries. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 2011, 158(3): R1. [10] Z. Rao, S. Wang,G. Zhang. Simulation and experiment of thermal energy management with phase change material for ageing LiFePO4 power battery. Energy Conversion and Management, 2011, 52(12): 3408-3414. [11] R. Kizilel, R. Sabbah, J. R. Selman, et al. An alternative cooling system to enhance the safety of Li-ion battery packs. Journal of Power Sources, 2009, 194(2): 1105-1112. [12] F. Samimi, A. Babapoor, M. Azizi, et al. Thermal management analysis of a Li-ion battery cell using phase change material loaded with carbon fibers. Energy, 2016, 96: 355-371. [13] X. Xiao, P. Zhang,M. Li. Preparation and thermal characterization of paraffin/metal foam composite phase change material. Applied Energy, 2013, 112: 1357-1366. [14] Alrashdan A, Mayyas A T, Al-Hallaj S. Thermo-mechanical behaviors of the expanded graphite-phase change material matrix used for thermal management of Li-ion battery packs. Journal of Materials Processing Technology, 2010, 210(1):174-179. [15] Z. G. Qu, W. Q. Li,W. Q. Tao. Numerical model of the passive thermal management system for high-power lithium ion battery by using porous metal foam saturated with phase change material. International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, 2014, 39(8): 3904-3913. [16] A. Greco, X. Jiang,D. Cao. An investigation of lithium-ion battery thermal management using paraffin/porous-graphite-matrix composite. Journal of Power Sources, 2015, 278: 50-68. f foam-PCM composite could preferably meet security temperature requirements at high discharge rate. 5. Conclusion A highly efficiency thermal management system using copper metal foam saturated with phase change materials is designed and experimentally investigated. The experimental results show that the thermal management mode of the foam-PCM composite reveals better performance of heat dissipation and temperature uniformity compared with air- cooling and pure PCM pattern. For instance, at 5C-dicharge rate, the max temperature for foam-PCM composite is much lower than that of pure PCM (47.86 Â°C v.s. 54.115 Â°C). By contrast, the foam-PCM composite also shows a slower Î”T-increasing rate during the discharge process, especially at high discharge rate. In addition, the dissipation mode of foam-PCM composite can effectively reduce the max temperature rise and difference and it can better meet security temperature requirements of high discharge rate. Acknowledgements The authors would like to thank for support of this work by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51376018). References [1] A. VÃ¤yrynen, J. Salminen. Lithium ion battery production. The Journal of Chemical Thermodynamics, 2012, 46: 80-85. [2] R. Kizilel, A. Lateef, R. Sabbah, et al. Passive control of temperature excursion and uniformity in high-energy Li-ion battery packs at high current and ambient temperature. Journal of Power Sources, 2008, 183(1): 370-375. [3] Ahmad A. Pesaran. Battery thermal models for hybrid vehicle simulations. Journal of Power Sources, 110 (2002) 377â€“382 [4] F. He,L. Ma. Thermal management of batteries employing active temperature control and reciprocating cooling flow. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer, 2015, 83: 164-172. [5] T. Wang, K. J. Tseng, J. Zhao, et al. Thermal investigation of lithium-ion battery module with different cell arrangement structures and forced air-cooling strategies. Applied Energy, 2014, 134: 229-238. [6] H. Park. head.ce:title: Experimental study of a passive thermal management system using copper foam-paraffin composite for lithium ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Thermal Engineering, School of Mechanical, Electronic and Control Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Flow and Heat Transfer of Phase Changing in Micro and Small Scale

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A passive thermal management system using copper metal foam saturated with phase change materials is designed for LiFePO4 battery pack. Copper metal foam is aimed to enhance low thermal conductivity of PCM and improve temperature uniformity of the battery pack. The thermal efficiency of the system is experimentally investigated and compared with two dissipation patterns of air cooling and pure PCM. The results show that the dissipation pattern of the foam-PCM composite can significantly reduce the battery’s surface temperature compared with the other two patterns. Moreover, the foam-PCM composite can bring the battery pack a better temperature uniformity, especially during the higher discharge rate.
